does: This paper systematically characterizes safety risks in large language models for medical applications by proposing a five-principle framework encompassing Truthfulness, Resilience, Fairness, Robustness, and Privacy with ten specific aspects. The authors develop MedGuard, a benchmark comprising 1,000 expert-verified medical questions designed to rigorously evaluate LLM safety rather than general medical knowledge. Evaluating eleven commonly used LLMs including ChatGPT, the study reveals substantial performance gaps compared to human physicians across most safety benchmarks. Despite claims of human-level performance on medical tasks, the results demonstrate that current models struggle with safety considerations, underscoring the gap between capability and trustworthiness in clinical contexts.
connects: This work builds on growing concerns about deploying large language models in high-stakes domains, extending existing medical AI safety literature that has focused primarily on accuracy metrics. It responds to optimistic reports claiming LLMs match physician performance by providing more nuanced safety-focused evaluation. The paper connects to broader AI safety frameworks emphasizing robustness, fairness, and privacy while adapting them specifically for medicine. It also relates to prior work on benchmark development for specialized domains and emerging discussion of alignment failures persisting even in safety-trained models, recognizing that general alignment mechanisms prove insufficient for medical applications.
enables: This framework and benchmark enable future work developing medical-specific safety alignment techniques and guardrails tailored to clinical needs. The comprehensive five-principle approach provides a foundation for safety standards in medical AI deployment and regulatory frameworks. The MedGuard benchmark itself becomes a tool for benchmarking improvements, allowing researchers to track progress toward trustworthy medical LLMs. The findings motivate investigation into which safety failures are inherent to transformer architectures versus remediable through better training or prompting strategies. This work also opens directions for human-in-the-loop systems leveraging LLM capabilities while maintaining physician oversight, and for developing domain-specific safety certification processes before clinical adoption.
